title: Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics — Security Review Prep (data classification, redaction, retention, access controls)
preview:
Doc purpose
This is a prep doc for Security Review of the Shadow Traffic tool for eval metrics ("shadow experiments"). This feature mirrors a sampled subset of production LLM requests to one or more shadow variants (candidate model/version/quantization/prompt) and computes quality metrics asynchronously. The control response is returned to the end user; shadow outputs are never returned.

We need security sign-off on:
- Data classification of all new artifacts (prompts, contexts, outputs, metadata, scores)
- Defaults for redaction + retention (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
- Access controls (Console + APIs), auditability, and data residency implications
- Threat model and mitigations (PII leakage, cross-tenant access, re-identification risk, prompt injection into eval pipeline)

Owner / reviewers
- Owner: Marcus Lin (Security Engineering)
- Primary reviewers: Kevin Osei (Audit/SIEM & retention controls), Naomi Feldman (Security/compliance product), Sean Gallagher (SRE), Logan Wright / Nadia Rahman (Telemetry), Benji Okafor (Control plane)

Meeting logistics (proposed)
- 60 minutes
- Agenda:
  1) Feature overview + data flows (10m)
  2) Data classification + proposed defaults (15m)
  3) Redaction + retention implementation details (15m)
  4) Access controls + audit logs (10m)
  5) Open questions / decisions / action items (10m)

Feature overview (security-relevant)
Shadow Traffic components:
- Control plane: defines Shadow Policy attached to routes/rollouts (sampling %, targets, headers/metadata behavior, retention knobs, fail-open)
- Data plane: mirrors sampled requests to shadow targets with strict timeouts; does not block control path
- Telemetry: emits shadow pair events (control + shadow metadata) with cardinality guardrails
- Storage: short-lived storage for redacted artifacts; aggregate metrics stored longer; sampled examples optional
- Scoring pipeline: async workers run rubric metrics + heuristics; outputs stored as scores + limited evidence
- Console: view experiments, deltas, and optionally drill into sampled examples

Key principle: fail-open + user-path isolation
- If shadow target, telemetry, or scoring is unavailable, the control request remains healthy and returns normally.

Data flow (simplified)
1) Request arrives (customer -> Redwood route)
2) Determine eligibility for shadowing via deterministic bucketing (fingerprint + policy sampling)
3) Control inference executes; response streamed/returned to user
4) Shadow inference executes in parallel (best-effort) and writes shadow result to shadow-ingestion pipeline
5) Telemetry emits a "shadow_pair" event referencing redacted artifact(s)
6) Scoring pipeline consumes artifacts, produces metric scores + aggregates
7) Console reads aggregates (and optional examples) subject to RBAC and audit logging

Data inventory + classification (proposed)
We should classify by (a) content sensitivity and (b) whether it is customer-provided.

A) Customer-provided content (highest risk)
1) Prompts / messages / tool arguments / retrieved context
- Source: end user or customer application
- Risk: PII, PHI, financial data, secrets, regulated data
- Proposed classification: Customer Confidential / Sensitive Content
- Storage default: do NOT store raw content by default for Hosted; allow opt-in for limited retention with redaction.

2) Model outputs (control and shadow)
- Source: generated; may contain verbatim user content or sensitive info
- Proposed classification: Customer Confidential / Sensitive Content
- Storage default: same as prompts (no raw content by default, only derived/aggregated metrics).

B) Metadata (moderate risk; potential re-identification)
3) Request identifiers (request_id, trace_id)
- Source: Redwood internal
- Risk: correlates with logs; could enable joining with customer traces
- Proposed classification: Internal Restricted
- Proposed handling: store as opaque IDs; do not expose raw IDs to customers; allow per-tenant internal debugging with elevated access only.

4) Fingerprints / bucketing keys
- Source: derived from request features
- Risk: if fingerprint stable and reversible/joinable, can leak information or enable linking
- Proposed classification: Internal Restricted
- Proposed handling:
  - Use keyed HMAC for fingerprint (tenant-scoped secret) rather than raw hash of content
  - Rotate key on defined cadence (proposal: 90 days) with backward compatibility for short-lived experiments only
  - Never store unhashed inputs used to compute fingerprint

5) Routing + model metadata (model_id, version, quant profile, region, route)
- Risk: limited; but could reveal customer deployment patterns
- Proposed classification: Internal
- Proposed exposure: customers see their own routing/variant labels (as configured); Redwood internal IDs hidden.

C) Derived data (lower risk but must avoid leakage)
6) Quality scores (rubric outputs, heuristic flags)
- Risk: may indirectly reveal sensitive content if we store rationales or excerpts
- Proposed classification: Customer Confidential (since tied to customer traffic)
- Proposed storage default: store numeric scores + minimal evidence; no free-form rationales that quote raw content by default.

7) Aggregates / dashboards (rates, deltas, confidence intervals)
- Risk: low, but still customer confidential
- Proposed classification: Customer Confidential
- Proposed retention: longer retention OK if aggregates only (no raw prompt/output).

What we store (proposed) — by artifact type
1) Raw prompt/output text
- Default: OFF (Hosted), configurable (Dedicated/Private)
- If ON: store redacted + truncated versions only (see Redaction)

2) Redacted prompt/output text (examples)
- Default: OFF (all tiers) except internal dogfood tenants
- If enabled by customer: store sampled examples up to N per day per route/experiment; enforce quotas

3) Token-level traces
- Default: OFF for shadow artifacts
- Rationale: very high sensitivity + volume; not required for v1 metrics

4) Scores + aggregates
- Default: ON
- Contains only numeric/enum outputs; optional short “reason codes” (e.g., FORMAT_ERROR, SAFETY_REFUSAL)

Redaction approach (proposed)
Goal: minimize retention of sensitive text while still enabling useful debugging.

Modes (per experiment / per route):
- Mode 0 (default): No content stored
  - Store only: metadata + scores + aggregates
  - Suitable for most Hosted customers

- Mode 1: Store redacted + truncated content for a small sampled subset
  - Store: redacted prompt+output snippets capped by bytes/tokens; remove/replace sensitive patterns
  - Use case: Dedicated beta customers who want example drill-down

- Mode 2 (Private only; optional): Customer-managed redaction allowlist
  - Redwood provides allowlist-based field retention (e.g., keep only system prompt template ID + tool names + numeric features)
  - Customer is responsible for ensuring no PII is included; Redwood enforces hard caps regardless

Redaction mechanics (v1)
- Hard truncation: max_prompt_chars and max_output_chars (proposal: 2,000 chars each) with clear UI warning
- Strip known secret formats:
  - API keys/tokens (common patterns), JWTs, OAuth tokens
  - Email addresses, phone numbers (regex-based)
  - Credit card numbers (Luhn-based detection)
  - SSNs (US pattern) (note: not comprehensive)
- Structured allowlist for known request schemas:
  - If request is structured (e.g., messages array), retain only role + length stats by default; optionally keep content after redaction in Mode 1
- Normalize whitespace; remove high-entropy strings (heuristic)
- Mark redaction provenance:
  - Fields: redaction_mode, redaction_version, redaction_actions_applied

Important: Redaction is best-effort
We must treat it as risk reduction, not a guarantee. Therefore, defaults should avoid storing content at all.

Retention (proposed defaults)
We need clear defaults + knobs per deployment mode.

Definitions
- “Artifacts”: any stored prompt/output text, even if redacted/truncated
- “Scores”: per-request rubric outputs
- “Aggregates”: time-windowed metrics (rates, deltas, CIs)

Hosted (default)
- Artifacts: 0 days (not stored)
- Scores: 7 days (for experiment computation + limited backfill)
- Aggregates: 30 days (for dashboard trend and comparisons)
- Notes:
  - Option to increase aggregates to 90 days on enterprise tier
  - Scores can be extended to 14 days for beta customers if compute allows

Dedicated (default)
- Artifacts: OFF by default; if enabled: 3 days
- Scores: 14 days
- Aggregates: 90 days
- Notes:
  - Dedicated customers often request longer retention; require explicit contract/security addendum for >14 days of scores or any artifacts beyond 3 days

Private (default)
- Artifacts: OFF by default; if enabled: 3 days
- Scores: 14 days
- Aggregates: customer-configurable (default 90 days)
- Notes:
  - For Private, customer can choose storage backend (within their VPC/on-prem) and retention is under their control; Redwood should still ship conservative defaults.

Deletion guarantees
- Implement TTL-based deletion for any per-request stored content and scores
- Make deletion “hard” (not just hidden) in primary store
- For logs/backups: document that backups may retain encrypted copies for X days (need confirmation from storage team; open question)

Data residency
- For Hosted/Dedicated: store artifacts/scores in the same region as the serving region when possible
- If scoring is centralized, ensure cross-region replication does not move sensitive artifacts unexpectedly
- For EU tenants: ensure any stored artifacts remain in EU region(s)
- Private: stays within customer environment

Access control / RBAC (proposed)
Objects
- Shadow Experiment (configuration)
- Shadow Results (aggregates)
- Shadow Scores (per-request scores)
- Shadow Examples (redacted prompt/output samples if enabled)

RBAC model
- Read access should be aligned with existing Console permissions for routes/rollouts:
  - Viewer: can view aggregates only
  - Operator: can create/modify experiments; view aggregates
  - Admin/Security: can enable content storage modes; view examples (if enabled)

Sensitive permission gates (explicit)
- A new permission: shadow_examples:read
  - Default: disabled for all roles
  - Must be explicitly granted per workspace
  - Requires org admin approval in Console (and is audited)

- A new permission: shadow_content_storage:enable
  - Allows enabling Mode 1/2 content retention
  - Default: disabled
  - Dedicated/Private: optionally enabled via contract + org policy

Internal Redwood access
- Support/engineering access to customer shadow artifacts must follow “break-glass”
  - Require ticket ID + time-limited approval
  - Log access in audit log
  - For Hosted: ideally no artifacts exist, minimizing need

Audit logging (requirements)
Actions to log (customer-visible where appropriate)
- Create/update/delete shadow experiment
- Change sampling %
- Change shadow targets
- Enable/disable content storage modes
- Change retention knobs
- Access to examples endpoint (who, when, how many examples retrieved)
- Export actions (CSV/JSON export from Console)

Log fields (minimum)
- actor_id, actor_type (user/service)
- workspace_id
- action
- object_id
- timestamp
- request_context (ip, user_agent)
- “reason” field for break-glass actions (free text)

Encryption / key management (proposed)
- In transit: TLS everywhere
- At rest:
  - Hosted/Dedicated: encrypt artifacts/scores with workspace-scoped keys (KMS)
  - Private: customer-managed keys (KMS/HSM) where available
- HMAC keys for fingerprinting:
  - Unique per workspace and per environment (prod vs staging)
  - Stored in secrets manager/KMS
  - Rotation policy: proposal 90 days (open question: impact to longitudinal comparability)

Threat model (summary)
1) PII leakage via stored prompts/outputs
- Mitigation: default no content storage; redaction+truncation; permission gates; TTL deletion

2) Cross-tenant data access (Console/API bug, storage partition bug)
- Mitigation: strict workspace scoping in service layer; integration tests; internal canary checks; periodic access reviews

3) Re-identification via stable fingerprint or identifiers
- Mitigation: tenant-scoped keyed HMAC; do not expose raw identifiers; rotate keys; avoid storing join keys that tie to raw request logs

4) Prompt injection / malicious content affecting scoring workers
- Mitigation: scoring workers treat content as untrusted; no dynamic code execution; sandboxed model-graded templates; output parsing hardened; strict timeouts

5) Excessive data exposure via exports or examples drill-down
- Mitigation: separate permissions; rate limits; maximum examples per day; redaction always applied; audit logs

6) Data residency violation (shadow artifacts copied across regions)
- Mitigation: region-aware storage + scoring; enforce residency policies at config validation time

7) Side-channel leakage via metrics (rare but possible)
- Mitigation: minimum aggregation thresholds (k-anonymity-ish guardrail); suppress slices with low sample counts; avoid high-cardinality labels

Cardinality controls (security + reliability)
- Disallow attaching user identifiers, full prompt IDs, or customer-provided arbitrary tags as dimensions
- Bucketed/hashed route IDs; safe enumerations for model/version
- Minimum sample size before showing segmented charts (proposal: hide segments where n < 200 requests or n < 50 scored pairs)

Proposed defaults (explicit list to confirm in review)
- Hosted: content storage disabled; only aggregates + scores retained (scores 7d, aggregates 30d)
- Dedicated: content storage disabled by default; if enabled: Mode 1 only, 3d retention
- Private: content storage disabled by default; if enabled: Mode 1 or Mode 2 with allowlist; 3d retention
- Examples require explicit permission shadow_examples:read
- Fingerprinting uses tenant-scoped HMAC; not reversible; no raw input stored
- Scoring stores numeric/enum outcomes; no free-form rationales quoting customer text by default

Unanswered questions (need decisions)
1) “Redaction guarantee” language in Console/docs
- Proposed: explicitly state redaction is best-effort and not a compliance boundary; recommend leaving artifacts OFF for regulated workloads unless using Private with customer-managed controls.
- Need: Security + Product wording approval.

2) Backups / log retention
- Do any stores used for artifacts/scores have backups that retain content beyond TTL?
- Need: confirmation from data storage owners + documented retention exception.

3) Break-glass policy for Dedicated incidents
- What is the minimum process for Redwood support to view examples when customer has enabled content storage?
- Proposed: ticket + manager approval + time-limited access + audit entry.
- Need: Support leadership + Security sign-off.

4) Data residency + centralized scoring
- Are we planning any centralized scoring workers that might pull artifacts cross-region?
- Proposed: scoring runs in-region when any artifacts are stored; if artifacts are OFF, scores can be computed from ephemeral in-memory payloads or region-local queues.
- Need: Architecture confirmation.

5) Fingerprint rotation vs experiment comparability
- If we rotate HMAC keys every 90 days, long-running experiments could see bucket assignment shifts.
- Proposed: pin fingerprint key version to experiment lifetime (max 30 days) and rotate outside experiment windows.
- Need: Platform decision.

6) Customer export endpoints
- Are we allowing export of per-request scores/examples via API in beta?
- Proposed: aggregates export only for beta; examples export later after hardened permissioning.
- Need: Product decision.

7) “Customer-managed redaction” in Private
- How do we validate allowlists to prevent accidental retention of sensitive fields?
- Proposed: Redwood only supports allowlists of known structured fields; no arbitrary JSON paths in v1.
- Need: Private deployment team input.

8) Model-graded evaluation storage
- If rubric uses a judge model that produces explanations, do we store that text?
- Proposed: store only numeric score + short reason code; discard judge rationale by default.
- Need: Applied ML confirmation.

9) Regulated tenants (HIPAA/PCI)
- Do we need a hard block preventing enabling content storage unless compliance flag is present?
- Proposed: for Hosted, if workspace has “regulated” tag, keep artifacts OFF and disable Mode 1 via UI.
- Need: Compliance product partner decision.

Concrete review asks (what we want from Security)
- Approval of default stance: “no content stored” for Hosted
- Approval of proposed RBAC gates and audit logging scope
- Approval of retention defaults and ability to configure Dedicated/Private with explicit opt-in
- Confirmation that keyed HMAC fingerprinting approach is acceptable and meets privacy expectations
- Guidance on any additional PII detectors required for v1 (or explicitly defer)

Implementation notes / current state (as of last edit)
- Audit logs: implemented for experiment create/update/delete and permissioned changes (see GitHub:redwood/pr-24966-audit-logs-for-shadow-experiment-changes)
- Redaction + retention pipeline: implemented basic truncation + regex-based detectors; TTL hooks added (see GitHub:redwood/pr-24980-data-retention-and-redaction-for-shadow-artifacts)
- Telemetry schema: includes redaction_version + content_stored boolean + retention_policy_id; dimensions reviewed for cardinality constraints (see Slack:eng-sre/1768063011-cardinality-risk-review-shadow-dimensions)

Action items
- [Kevin Osei] Confirm backup retention semantics for artifact store and whether TTL deletion is sufficient (due: 2026-02-07)
- [Nadia Rahman] Document region placement for queues/workers; confirm no cross-region pulls when artifacts enabled (due: 2026-02-07)
- [Jonas Weber] Confirm judge rationale storage behavior; update scoring output schema to avoid free-form text (due: 2026-02-06)
- [Benji Okafor] Add config validation: block enabling examples without shadow_examples:read gate; block Mode 1 unless enterprise feature enabled (due: 2026-02-10)
- [Naomi Feldman] Provide recommended customer-facing wording for data retention + redaction disclaimers (due: 2026-02-10)

Appendix: Proposed customer-facing summary (draft)
- Shadow Traffic lets you measure quality deltas by scoring shadow outputs asynchronously.
- By default, Redwood does not store prompt/output content; we store only metrics.
- You can opt in to storing a small number of redacted examples for debugging (Dedicated/Private only by default), with short retention and strict permissions.
- Retention is configurable; defaults are conservative.

Appendix: Notes to self (internal)
- Keep pressure on “examples” scope creep. The safest default is metrics-only.
- Ensure examples endpoints are isolated and audited; treat as sensitive as logs.
- Confirm we never include raw prompt text in telemetry labels or metric tags (common footgun).

- object_id
- timestamp

3) Re-identification via stable fingerprint or identifiers
- Mitigation: tenant-scoped keyed HMAC; do not expose raw identifiers; rotate keys; avoid storing join keys that tie to raw request logs

4) Prompt injection / malicious content affecting scoring workers
- Mitigation: scoring workers treat content as untrusted; no dynamic code execution; sandboxed model-graded templates; output parsing hardened; strict timeouts

5) Excessive data exposure via exports or examples drill-down
- Mitigation: separate permissions; rate limits; maximum examples per day; redaction always applied; audit logs

6) Data residency violation (shadow artifacts copied across regions)
- Mitigation: region-aware storage + scoring; enforce residency policies at config validation time

7) Side-channel leakage via metrics (rare but possible)
- Mitigation: minimum aggregation thresholds (k-anonymity-ish guardrail); suppress slices with low sample counts; avoid high-cardinality labels

Cardinality controls (security + reliability)
- Disallow attaching user identifiers, full prompt IDs, or customer-provided arbitrary tags as dimensions
- Bucketed/hashed route IDs; safe enumerations for model/version
- Minimum sample size before showing segmented charts (proposal: hide segments where n < 200 requests or n < 50 scored pairs)

Proposed defaults (explicit list to confirm in review)
- Hosted: content storage disabled; only aggregates + scores retained (scores 7d, aggregates 30d)
- Dedicated: content storage disabled by default; if enabled: Mode 1 only, 3d retention
- Private: content storage disabled by default; if enabled: Mode 1 or Mode 2 with allowlist; 3d retention
- Examples require explicit permission shadow_examples:read
- Fingerprinting uses tenant-scoped HMAC; not reversible; no raw input stored
- Scoring stores numeric/enum outcomes; no free-form rationales quoting customer text by default

Unanswered questions (need decisions)
1) “Redaction guarantee” language in Console/docs
- Proposed: explicitly state redaction is best-effort and not a compliance boundary; recommend leaving artifacts OFF for regulated workloads unless using Private with customer-managed controls.
- Need: Security + Product wording approval.

2) Backups / log retention
- Do any stores used for artifacts/scores have backups that retain content beyond TTL?
- Need: confirmation from data storage owners + documented retention exception.

3) Break-glass policy for Dedicated incidents
- What is the minimum process for Redwood support to view examples when customer has enabled content storage?
- Proposed: ticket + manager approval + time-limited access + audit entry.
- Need: Support leadership + Security sign-off.

4) Data residency + centralized scoring
- Are we planning any centralized scoring workers that might pull artifacts cross-region?
- Proposed: scoring runs in-region when any artifacts are stored; if artifacts are OFF, scores can be computed from ephemeral in-memory payloads or region-local queues.
- Need: Architecture confirmation.

5) Fingerprint rotation vs experiment comparability
- If we rotate HMAC keys every 90 days, long-running experiments could see bucket assignment shifts.
- Proposed: pin fingerprint key version to experiment lifetime (max 30 days) and rotate outside experiment windows.
- Need: Platform decision.

6) Customer export endpoints
- Are we allowing export of per-request scores/examples via API in beta?
- Proposed: aggregates export only for beta; examples export later after hardened permissioning.
- Need: Product decision.

7) “Customer-managed redaction” in Private
- How do we validate allowlists to prevent accidental retention of sensitive fields?
- Proposed: Redwood only supports allowlists of known structured fields; no arbitrary JSON paths in v1.
- Need: Private deployment team input.

8) Model-graded evaluation storage
- If rubric uses a judge model that produces explanations, do we store that text?
- Proposed: store only numeric score + short reason code; discard judge rationale by default.
- Need: Applied ML confirmation.

9) Regulated tenants (HIPAA/PCI)
- Do we need a hard block preventing enabling content storage unless compliance flag is present?
- Proposed: for Hosted, if workspace has “regulated” tag, keep artifacts OFF and disable Mode 1 via UI.
- Need: Compliance product partner decision.

Concrete review asks (what we want from Security)
- Approval of default stance: “no content stored” for Hosted
- Approval of proposed RBAC gates and audit logging scope
- Approval of retention defaults and ability to configure Dedicated/Private with explicit opt-in
- Confirmation that keyed HMAC fingerprinting approach is acceptable and meets privacy expectations
- Guidance on any additional PII detectors required for v1 (or explicitly defer)

Implementation notes / current state (as of last edit)
- Audit logs: implemented for experiment create/update/delete and permissioned changes (see GitHub:redwood/pr-24966-audit-logs-for-shadow-experiment-changes)
- Redaction + retention pipeline: implemented basic truncation + regex-based detectors; TTL hooks added (see GitHub:redwood/pr-24980-data-retention-and-redaction-for-shadow-artifacts)
- Telemetry schema: includes redaction_version + content_stored boolean + retention_policy_id; dimensions reviewed for cardinality constraints (see Slack:eng-sre/1768063011-cardinality-risk-review-shadow-dimensions)

Action items
- [Kevin Osei] Confirm backup retention semantics for artifact store and whether TTL deletion is sufficient (due: 2026-02-07)
- [Nadia Rahman] Document region placement for queues/workers; confirm no cross-region pulls when artifacts enabled (due: 2026-02-07)
- [Jonas Weber] Confirm judge rationale storage behavior; update scoring output schema to avoid free-form text (due: 2026-02-06)
- [Benji Okafor] Add config validation: block enabling examples without shadow_examples:read gate; block Mode 1 unless enterprise feature enabled (due: 2026-02-10)
- [Naomi Feldman] Provide recommended customer-facing wording for data retention + redaction disclaimers (due: 2026-02-10)

Appendix: Proposed customer-facing summary (draft)
- Shadow Traffic lets you measure quality deltas by scoring shadow outputs asynchronously.
- By default, Redwood does not store prompt/output content; we store only metrics.
- You can opt in to storing a small number of redacted examples for debugging (Dedicated/Private only by default), with short retention and strict permissions.
- Retention is configurable; defaults are conservative.

Appendix: Notes to self (internal)
- Keep pressure on “examples” scope creep. The safest default is metrics-only.
- Ensure examples endpoints are isolated and audited; treat as sensitive as logs.
- Confirm we never include raw prompt text in telemetry labels or metric tags (common footgun).

title: Break-glass policy draft: Offline bundle signature verification override (Redwood Private on-prem/air-gapped)
preview:
Document purpose
This doc defines draft policy language and an approval workflow for using a break-glass override when Redwood Private is being installed/upgraded from an offline bundle and normal cryptographic verification cannot be completed.

This is intended for:
- Air-gapped / offline customer environments (VPC w/o outbound or true on-prem air-gap)
- Installer verification failures where the bundle is otherwise believed to be legitimate but verification is blocked by environmental constraints

This is NOT intended to:
- Replace signed bundles as the default
- Create a routine “ignore verification” path
- Allow shipping/consuming unsigned bundles except under the separate exception process for emergency hotfixes

Status / scope
- Draft for pilot + early GA readiness.
- Applies to Redwood Private on-prem installer and associated preflight tooling.
- Covers verification of the offline bundle manifest signature and integrity checks.

Definitions
- “Normal verification”: Installer preflight validates (a) signature over canonical manifest, (b) manifest hash list matches bundle contents, and (c) signing key is trusted by the installer trust store.
- “Break-glass”: A time-bound, explicitly approved override that permits install/upgrade to proceed when normal verification cannot complete. This override must be auditable and must not silently downgrade security.
- “Customer”: Operator/administrator in the customer environment installing Redwood Private.
- “Redwood approvers”: Internal Redwood staff who can approve break-glass use.

Policy statement (draft)
1) Default behavior: fail closed
- The installer MUST fail closed on signature verification errors and MUST NOT proceed by default.
- Any override path MUST be explicit (flag/setting) and produce strong warnings.

2) Break-glass is allowed only for verified customer identity + approved use
Break-glass MAY be used only when all of the following are true:
- Customer identity is verified through an active support channel (support ticket + account validation).
- The offline bundle origin is traced to an official Redwood release artifact channel OR a Redwood-controlled transfer mechanism.
- An incident record exists (Support ticket) describing the verification failure and attempted remediation steps.
- Redwood approval is granted per the approval matrix below.

3) Break-glass MUST be time-bound and minimally permissive
- Override should be scoped to a single installation attempt and single bundle identifier (build_id / bundle_id) where possible.
- Installer must emit an audit event indicating break-glass was used, including reason code and required context.
- Break-glass must not disable integrity checks that can still be performed offline (e.g., file hashes) unless explicitly documented in the request and approved.

4) Break-glass is an exception; follow-up is mandatory
- After a break-glass install, Redwood and the customer must execute post-incident remediation steps to return to normal verification for subsequent upgrades.
- Repeated break-glass use on the same environment (more than once per quarter) requires Security Eng review and a written remediation plan.

Allowed break-glass scenarios (draft)
A. Trust root not yet deployed / key rotation mismatch
- Example: customer installer trust store lacks the new signing key after rotation (unknown key).
- Preferred fix: update trust store using documented offline mechanism and retry.
- Break-glass only if: customer cannot update trust store in time due to change control window constraints AND the bundle can be verified by Redwood out-of-band (see “Required evidence”).

B. Environmental time skew causing validity window check failures
- Example: air-gapped environment has clock skew, signature “not yet valid” in the local environment.
- Preferred fix: correct time source, retry; optional diagnostic time override for validation only.
- Break-glass only if: customer cannot correct time within the change window, and Redwood confirms bundle signature validity in a known-good environment.

C. Verification tooling incompatibility / known installer defect
- Example: a known bug in a specific installer version causes false verification failures.
- Preferred fix: upgrade installer to minimum fixed version.
- Break-glass only if: upgrading installer is not feasible within the maintenance window and risk is accepted with explicit approvals.

D. Bundle transport corruption suspected but not confirmed
- Generally NOT eligible for break-glass. If corruption is suspected, customer must re-transfer bundle and re-run verification.
- Only eligible if: there is strong independent evidence the bundle is intact (hashes from multiple independent transfer steps match), and Security Eng explicitly approves.

Not allowed (hard “NO”) scenarios
- Customer cannot identify where the bundle came from.
- Bundle was provided by a third party not controlled by Redwood.
- Evidence suggests manifest mismatch or tampering (hash mismatch between manifest and content).
- Customer requests break-glass as a standing practice (“always skip verification”).
- Customer requests bypass to install an older version (downgrade) without documented rollback procedures.

Approval matrix (draft)
Break-glass request types and required approvals:
1) Low-risk (procedural/time skew, documented workaround exists)
- Required: Support Readiness Lead (Dev Patel) OR on-call Support escalation (Owen Phillips)
- Required: Solutions Lead for account (Fatima Noor) for customer confirmation (optional for existing enterprise accounts if Support can validate)
- Notification: Security Eng (Marcus Lin) within 1 business day

2) Medium-risk (unknown key/trust store mismatch; installer defect workaround)
- Required: Support Readiness Lead (Dev Patel)
- Required: Release Engineering Lead (Connor O'Brien) OR Installer Eng Lead (Ethan Park)
- Required: Security Eng sign-off (Marcus Lin)

3) High-risk (any case involving inability to perform remaining integrity checks; repeated break-glass; unclear provenance)
- Required: Security Eng sign-off (Marcus Lin)
- Required: Security executive sponsor approval (Dr. Aisha Rahman)
- Required: GRC review for evidence capture (Olga Petrov) if customer is regulated / requires formal attestation
- Required: Product owner notification (Vivek Kulkarni)

Notes:
- Approvals may be given in writing via Jira/Support ticket comments or email, but must be captured as permanent ticket artifacts.
- Slack approvals are not sufficient unless copied into the ticket.

Required evidence (what must be collected)
All break-glass requests MUST attach:
- Customer account + environment identifiers (as allowed by customer policy)
- Bundle identifier(s): bundle_id/build_id, version, and any manifest file name/version
- Installer version and platform details (OS, Kubernetes version if known)
- Full preflight output including verification error codes
- Output from “inspect bundle” command (if available) or equivalent manifest print
- Hashes:
  - SHA-256 of the bundle archive file as transported
  - SHA-256 of manifest file as extracted
- Source chain:
  - How the bundle was obtained (portal download, SE transfer, etc.) and who handled it
- Requested maintenance window end time (so approval can be time-bound)

Additionally required for medium/high-risk:
- Redwood-side verification record: internal verification run in a known-good environment using the expected public key(s) and canonicalization rules
- If the failure is unknown key: confirmation the bundle is signed by an active Redwood signing key (not revoked)

Workflow (draft)
1) Customer hits verification failure
- Customer runs installer preflight (or “inspect bundle”) and captures output.
- Support/SE opens or links to a Support ticket. (Example category: “Private install → Offline bundle verification failure”.)

2) Triage and remediation attempt (mandatory)
- Confirm the customer is using the required installer version for the bundle.
- Confirm trust store status and documented update steps.
- Confirm offline environment time is reasonable (if failure indicates validity window issues).
- Confirm bundle integrity: re-check transport hash; ensure no re-packaging steps altered content.
- If remediation succeeds, break-glass is not used.

3) Break-glass request submission
Support/SE fills out a “Break-glass request” template in the ticket:
- Reason category (A/B/C/D)
- Evidence checklist (attached artifacts)
- Proposed scope (single bundle_id, single attempt)
- Proposed flags/config to use (exact command line)
- Risk statement + customer acknowledgment status

4) Approvals
- Route to required approvers per matrix.
- Approver must explicitly state: “Approved break-glass for bundle_id X, version Y, for customer Z, valid until <timestamp>, scope <install/upgrade>, reason <category>.”

5) Execution
- Customer executes installer with the break-glass override flag.
- Installer MUST:
  - Print prominent warning and require interactive confirmation (or documented non-interactive equivalent with explicit acknowledgment variable)
  - Emit local audit event(s) (see “Audit requirements”)
  - Record a local state marker that break-glass was used for that install (for later support + compliance)

6) Post-incident follow-up
- Within 5 business days (or sooner for regulated accounts), Redwood provides:
  - Root cause summary (time skew, trust store out-of-date, bug, etc.)
  - Remediation steps to restore normal verification for next upgrade
  - If a defect: target version containing fix + backport plan if needed
- Close-out requirements:
  - Ticket must contain the final commands used and confirmation of install success
  - If high-risk, Security Eng writes a short internal postmortem note in the ticket

Break-glass override mechanics (draft requirements for engineering)
This section is intentionally “policy + product requirements” to ensure the implementation supports auditability.
- The override must be gated behind a flag that is hard to accidentally set (e.g., --break-glass-allow-unverified-bundle).
- The override must not be a silent config default.
- The installer should still perform as many checks as possible, and only bypass the failing check (e.g., signature chain validation), depending on the reason category.
- The installer should require a reason code parameter (e.g., --break-glass-reason=TIME_SKEW) to standardize reporting.
- The installer should encourage/require a “break-glass ticket id” field for audit correlation (e.g., --break-glass-ticket=SUP-xxxxx).

Audit requirements (local logs / export)
When break-glass is used, installer must log locally:
- Event name: bundle_verification_break_glass_used
- Timestamp (local)
- Bundle identifiers: bundle_id/build_id, bundle version
- Installer version
- Failure reason code (original verification error) + operator-provided reason code
- Whether integrity checks passed/failed (hash verification status)
- Request correlation id: support ticket id (if provided)
- Redaction rules: never include customer secret material; avoid absolute file paths if customer considers them sensitive (configurable).

If audit-log exporter is configured, the event should be exportable to SIEM under the existing schema.

Customer communication (draft language)
Support/SE should use the following language (adapt as needed):
- “Redwood’s default posture is to block installs when signature verification fails. We can provide a time-bound break-glass override only as an exception, with approval, and only after we collect required evidence to reduce supply-chain risk. This will generate local audit markers so your organization can track the exception.”
- “This does not change our recommendation: the environment should be remediated so future upgrades verify normally.”

Risk acknowledgement
For medium/high-risk break-glass approvals, obtain customer acknowledgment (in ticket/email):
- Customer confirms they understand signature verification is not fully enforced for this install.
- Customer confirms they will remediate (trust store update / time correction / installer upgrade) before the next upgrade window.

Open questions (needs alignment)
- Exact list of checks that can be selectively bypassed. (We should avoid a “bypass everything” mode.)
- Whether break-glass should require a one-time token generated by Redwood (offline-friendly?) vs purely policy gated. (Current bias: no token in v1 due to air-gap constraints; revisit for v2.)
- Downgrade/rollback interplay: should break-glass ever allow rollback to older versions if rollback is signed but blocked due to trust store mismatch?
- How we handle customers with strict change controls that prevent rapid trust store updates.

Related docs / next steps
- Align with Confluence exception process for unsigned hotfix bundles (different pathway; do not conflate).
- Ensure installer implementation matches policy (flag naming, required ticket id, event emission).
- Run a tabletop exercise with Support + Release Eng + Security for a simulated key rotation mismatch scenario.

Changelog (informal)
- 2026-01-30: Added approval matrix tiers + required evidence list; clarified “not allowed” scenarios.
- 2026-01-12: Drafted initial workflow and customer comms language based on pilot feedback.
- 2025-12-18: Initial outline created from Slack policy wording thread + threat model follow-ups.

title: Offline bundle signing – threat model brainstorm notes (working)
preview:
Context / why this exists
- We need to support fully offline/air-gapped installs of Redwood Private.
- Customer installs should be able to verify provenance + integrity of the entire offline bundle without any network access.
- The "formal" threat model will be written in Confluence; this doc is the scratchpad / brainstorming source.

In-scope assets (things we care about protecting)
- Offline bundle contents (examples, not exhaustive):
  - Installer binary / CLI + embedded configs
  - Helm charts / manifests
  - Container image references (and/or image tarballs if included)
  - Runtime/model artifacts, weights, tokenizer files, etc.
  - SBOM file(s) or pointers + checksums
  - Manifest describing bundle contents (version, build id, hashes)
  - Signature over manifest (detached or embedded)
  - Trust roots (public keys) that the installer uses for verification
- Build pipeline outputs:
  - The exact set of artifacts that are considered "the release"
  - The signing step + logs
- Local install state:
  - Previously installed version record used for downgrade prevention
  - Local audit logs/events about verification outcomes

Trust boundaries / threat surfaces
- Redwood build + release pipeline boundary
  - Who/what can influence artifact inputs?
  - Who can sign?
- Artifact publication boundary
  - Where bundles are stored pre-delivery (internal object store, release share)
- Customer transport boundary (high-risk)
  - Bundles moved via USB, secure courier, DVD, etc.
  - Unzip tools, line-ending conversions, antivirus “cleaning”, partial transfers
- Customer install boundary
  - On-prem installer verifies signature + hashes
  - Cluster admission/helm apply
- Post-install boundary
  - Day-2: upgrades, rollbacks, support bundles, audit export

Assumptions (need to confirm)
- Installer is allowed to fail closed (block install) on verification errors by default.
- Some customers will have unreliable time sources (no NTP; manual clocks; time skew).
- Customers may have constraints on crypto: FIPS mode, disallowing certain curves/algorithms.
- Customers might demand evidence: "who signed", "where is the root of trust", "how do you rotate".

Attacker profiles (who/what are we defending against)
- Opportunistic tampering during transport (malicious or accidental)
- Malware on a machine that touches the bundle (pre-install workstation) that can edit files
- Insider threat at Redwood: developer/release operator trying to ship a backdoored bundle
- Compromise of CI runner / build environment
- Compromise of signing key material (catastrophic)
- Customer-side malicious admin (less relevant; they can always bypass if root) BUT we still want strong auditing + safe defaults
- "Downgrade" attacker: pushes older but signed bundle to reintroduce known vuln
- "Confused deputy" via support / break-glass path abuse

Primary goals (security properties)
- Integrity: detect any modification of bundle contents.
- Authenticity/provenance: installer can verify bundle is signed by a Redwood-trusted signing key.
- Anti-replay/downgrade: prevent installing older signed bundles where policy disallows.
- Auditability: produce local evidence of verification + any overrides.
- Resilience: don’t brick customers during key rotation; support multiple keys.

Non-goals (likely)
- Confidentiality of artifacts inside the bundle (bundles are not encrypted). If customer needs encryption-at-rest during transport, that’s separate (e.g., customer-managed encryption).
- Preventing a fully privileged customer admin from installing whatever they want (but we should make bypass explicit and logged).

Attack tree brainstorm (rough)
Root: Customer ends up running a compromised/unauthorized Redwood Private deployment

1) Tamper with bundle contents
  1.1 Modify installer binary
    - Replace binary with trojan, keep filename
    - Patch verification routine to always pass
  1.2 Modify Helm chart/manifests
    - Add malicious init container / privileged pod
    - Change image registries/tags to attacker-controlled
    - Add cluster-admin bindings
  1.3 Modify container images (if images shipped as tar)
    - Swap layers / introduce backdoor
  1.4 Modify model/runtime artifacts
    - Insert payload in runtime plugin
    - Replace weights with watermarked/poisoned version (integrity goal; safety/quality risk)
  1.5 Modify manifest
    - Remove entries so modified artifact is unchecked
    - Swap hash algorithm identifier

2) Trick verification into accepting tampered content
  2.1 Canonicalization mismatch
    - Create a different JSON serialization that verifies under one tool but parses differently in installer
    - Unicode confusables / whitespace / key ordering edge cases
  2.2 Path traversal / symlink tricks inside bundle
    - Hash computed over one file, installer reads another (symlink swap)
    - Zip slip style extraction leading to overwritten trust store
  2.3 Hash collision / weak hash algorithm selection
    - Choose weak digest algorithm (avoid SHA1/MD5)
  2.4 Verification bypass via "break-glass" flag
    - Social engineering Support / customer operator to use override permanently

3) Replay / downgrade
  3.1 Install old signed bundle
    - Bundle is legitimate signature but contains known CVEs / old images
  3.2 Roll back local install state
    - Delete/modify local record so downgrade checks pass

4) Key / trust compromise
  4.1 Signing private key stolen
    - Attacker produces fully valid signed bundles
  4.2 Untrusted public key inserted into trust store
    - Modify installer trust store file in bundle or on disk
  4.3 Build pipeline signs wrong artifacts
    - "Time-of-check" vs "time-of-use" mismatch: manifest generated, then artifacts swapped before signing/publish

5) CI/CD compromise and build provenance gaps
  5.1 Compromised runner injects payload post-tests
  5.2 Dependency compromise (bundle tooling)
  5.3 Human error in release process
    - Signs staging build and publishes as prod

6) Time-based validation failures that cause unsafe workarounds
  6.1 Cert not-yet-valid / expired due to clock skew
    - Customer forced to use override, lowering security

Likely mitigations / design levers (brain dump)
- Sign a single canonical manifest that includes:
  - Bundle version + build_id
  - Hashes (SHA-256 minimum) for every included artifact
  - Explicit relative paths (normalized) and file sizes
  - Possibly a content-type field per artifact (installer/helm/image/model/sbom)
  - SBOM filename + hash (or pointer + hash)
  - Created_at timestamp (careful w/ time skew)
- Detached signature file (manifest.sig) + explicit envelope format
  - Avoid ambiguity: signature covers EXACT canonical bytes of manifest
  - Define canonicalization in spec/ADR (JSON canonicalization rules)
- Trust store strategy
  - Installer ships with embedded trusted public keys (or a trust store file packaged with installer)
  - Support multiple active keys (for rotation)
  - Pin key ids + allowlist
- Key ceremony + custody
  - HSM-backed keys where feasible
  - Two-person approval for signing operations (at least for key usage / release promotion)
  - Audit logs from KMS/HSM and CI
- Reproducible bundle builds (deterministic) where possible
  - Stable ordering of manifest entries
  - Avoid embedding local timestamps into artifacts unless captured as metadata fields and explicitly signed
- Verification behavior
  - Fail closed by default on: invalid signature, unknown key, manifest parse/canonicalization error, hash mismatch
  - Clear error codes and remediation guidance to reduce support pressure for unsafe bypass
- Downgrade prevention
  - Signed version metadata and local "installed version" state
  - Policy: allow rollback only to versions within a signed/approved window (e.g., last N versions) OR require explicit override with audit record
- Break-glass override
  - Should exist for operational continuity but should be gated:
    - require an explicit flag
    - produce loud console warnings
    - emit a local audit event "verification_bypassed"
    - optionally require a "reason" string
    - document approval path (Support + Security/Release)
- Local audit logging
  - On verify success/failure: capture key_id, bundle build_id, manifest digest, failure reason category
  - Ensure logs avoid sensitive data (no full paths if they include customer-specific details? maybe ok; TBD)
- Transport hardening guidance
  - Provide customer guidance for checksum verification post-transfer
  - Recommend tar over zip? Or provide a single bundle file format with predictable extraction
  - Warn about CRLF conversions / "smart" unzip tools
- Symlink and path traversal defenses
  - During bundle build: disallow symlinks in artifacts directory
  - During verification: resolve paths, ensure all paths stay within bundle root
  - Hash the extracted file bytes actually used
- Reduce reliance on wall clock
  - Prefer non-expiring signatures (key rotation handled by trust store updates) OR long validity windows
  - If using certs with validity: provide a controlled time source mechanism (e.g., allow passing explicit current time for diagnostics only)

Questions / open items to validate with Security Eng + Release Eng
- Crypto + envelope:
  - Are we using raw public keys (ed25519) vs X.509 cert chain?
  - If X.509: do we require full chain shipped in bundle? Where do roots live?
  - If ed25519: how do we represent key ids and rotate? (key id = hash of public key?)
  - Do we need FIPS mode compatibility? ed25519 may be problematic for some customers; ECDSA P-256 might be required.
  - Do we need dual-signing during transition/rotation (two signatures)?
- Time handling:
  - Are signatures time-bound? If yes, how do we handle air-gapped clock skew? Any "not-before" issues.
  - If not time-bound, how do we meet customer requirements for revocation? (revocation is hard offline)
- Revocation model:
  - If a signing key is compromised, how do we prevent acceptance in air-gapped envs that can’t receive updates?
  - Do we rely on trust store update distribution as the revocation mechanism?
  - Can we ship an emergency "trust store update" bundle to customers? What’s the operational plan?
- Bundle contents:
  - Are we signing image tarballs or just references? If just references, offline install still needs images somewhere.
  - If images are shipped, do we verify image digests and/or OCI layout signatures?
- Provenance evidence:
  - Do we need SLSA-ish provenance statements for customers? Or just internal evidence (GRC)?
  - What evidence must be kept for audits (build logs, signing logs, approvals)? Where stored and for how long?
- Failure mode UX:
  - What error taxonomy do we want (hash mismatch vs signature invalid vs unknown key vs parse/canonicalization error)?
  - What should be the message for "unknown key" during rotation (actionable: update installer trust store / upgrade installer)?
- Break-glass policy:
  - Who can approve? How do we communicate to customer? How do we ensure follow-up remediation?

Customer-driven constraints heard so far (from SE / calls)
- Air-gapped means no outbound network; sometimes even no internal NTP.
- Security teams want: "Show me your key ceremony", "Where are keys stored", "How do you rotate", "How do I audit that verification happened".
- Some customers require ability to export audit logs to SIEM later (even if offline at install time).
- Transport is messy: bundles often copied via Windows workstations or scanning tools that can alter files.

Potential abuse cases to explicitly call out in final threat model
- "Support tells customer to use break-glass" becomes a default workaround. Need guardrails + training.
- Key rotation that leaves older air-gapped sites stranded -> they disable verification entirely.
- Manifest canonicalization disagreements across versions -> signature verification failures -> customers request disabling checks.

Notes to incorporate into formal mitigations list
- Define canonical JSON rules once and test across languages (Go/Rust/Python) if applicable.
- Add integration tests for tampered bundles (modified manifest, swapped artifact, truncated bundle).
- Consider a separate "bundle inspect" command for Support/SE to diagnose quickly and reduce pressure to bypass.
- Make downgrade prevention explicit: signed "min_installer_version" and/or "min_bundle_version"? TBD.

Action items (rough)
- Marcus: sync with Connor + Sahana on CI threat scenarios (TOCTOU, signing wrong artifacts).
- Kevin: propose audit event schema fields + redaction guidance.
- Hanae: confirm key storage options + what’s feasible for FIPS customers.
- Ethan: confirm installer verification flow, especially symlink/path traversal handling.
- Olga/Daniel: list controls/evidence mapping needed for customer security reviews.

Appendix: quick checklist of "must answer" for threat model doc
- What’s the root of trust?
- What exactly is signed?
- How is canonicalization defined?
- How do we handle key rotation + compromise in offline environments?
- What do we log locally and how can it be exported?
- What are the break-glass conditions and approval flow?
- What is the downgrade policy?

title: Template Security Review Notes (Quickstart Templates v1)
preview:
Doc purpose
- Capture security review notes for the Quickstart Templates (RAG + Agents) and template CLI before public launch.
- Track decisions + required actions for secrets handling, sample data, logging redaction, telemetry privacy, and Secure SDLC compliance.

Context / scope
- In scope (v1 launch):
  - Python RAG+agent template
  - TypeScript RAG+agent template
  - Template CLI (init + doctor)
  - Local dev OTel collector compose + example exporters
  - README / docs guidance that ships with templates
- Out of scope (v1):
  - Multi-tenant production collector deployment guidance beyond minimal notes
  - Enterprise-specific KMS/HSM integration examples (docs can mention, but don’t implement)

Review sessions
- 2025-02-14: initial review kickoff (DevEx + Security)
  - Attendees: Marcus Lin, Monica Patel, Kaitlyn Nguyen, Hanae Suzuki
- 2025-02-24: follow-up on redaction + telemetry tagging approach
  - Attendees: Marcus Lin, Allison Grant, Monica Patel, Wei Chen
- 2025-03-07: supply chain + CI gates review
  - Attendees: Marcus Lin, Sahana Iyer, Monica Patel

High-level outcome
- Status: Conditional approval pending closure of P0/P1 items below.
- Summary: No fundamental blockers to shipping templates, but we must tighten defaults and docs to avoid: (a) accidental secret exposure via logs/traces, (b) users copying insecure patterns from sample code, and (c) dependency drift and unscanned updates.

Threat model (quick pass)
- Primary risks:
  1) Secrets exposure (API keys, tokens, cookies) via logs, tracing attributes, crash dumps, or CLI output.
  2) PII leakage into telemetry (prompt, retrieved docs, tool inputs/outputs) via logs/traces/metrics.
  3) Supply chain issues: template dependencies + transitive deps pulling in known CVEs or compromised packages.
  4) Unsafe sample data / sample documents that appear “real” and get re-shared.
  5) “Doctor” command making outbound requests and printing overly-detailed errors (headers, full URLs, tokens).
- Non-goals (for v1): preventing users from logging their own data in their app. We can provide strong defaults, warnings, and safe scaffolding.

Findings and actions

P0 (must fix before public launch)
1) Secrets in logs (explicit + implicit)
- Observation:
  - Several error paths (HTTP client exceptions, SDK errors) can include request headers or full exception text. Some libraries include headers by default if not carefully handled.
  - In early internal beta, at least one stack trace printed config values when env var parsing failed.
- Required action:
  - Implement a redaction filter at the logger layer that:
    - Redacts values for keys matching common secret patterns: *key, *token, authorization, cookie, set-cookie, x-api-key, bearer, password, secret.
    - Redacts values that match likely API key formats (heuristic/regex) in free-form strings.
  - Ensure exception logging uses structured fields and does NOT dump entire request objects.
  - Ensure CLI never prints environment variable values; only prints presence/absence and last-4 (optional) for debugging.
- Owner / tracking:
  - Monica Patel + Dana Lo (implementation), Marcus Lin (review)
  - Reference: github:redwood-quickstarts/pr-102-add-otel-instrumentation-and-log-redaction

2) OTel tracing attributes may contain PII
- Observation:
  - Template instrumentation initially included "prompt" and "retrieved_context" in span attributes for debugging.
  - This is not acceptable as a default. Even with local dev, users will copy/paste.
- Required action:
  - Remove all raw prompt, completion, retrieved document content, and tool payloads from default traces.
  - Replace with safe metadata only:
    - model name/version, request_id, region, http status, retry_count, streaming_reconnects
    - token counts (input/output), latency breakdown
    - retrieval stats (num_chunks, top_k, similarity score summary) WITHOUT content
    - tool name, tool success/failure, tool duration; tool argument keys allowed but values redacted
  - Provide an explicit opt-in debug mode (REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_DEBUG_TELEMETRY=true) that still does NOT record raw content by default, but can add limited hashes/lengths.
- Notes:
  - If we ever add “prompt hash”, it must be salted per-process, not stable across runs, and documented clearly.
- Owner / tracking:
  - Allison Grant (standards), Monica Patel (template changes), Marcus Lin (sign-off)

3) Template telemetry tagging must be opt-in
- Observation:
  - Adoption KPI plan includes tagging template-originated traffic via headers/tags.
  - Some corporate proxies reject unknown headers; also privacy review requires explicit user consent.
- Required action:
  - Telemetry header/tagging must be:
    - Disabled by default.
    - Explicitly enabled via env var (e.g., REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_TELEMETRY_OPT_IN=true).
    - Documented in README as “optional, non-sensitive, used for product metrics”.
  - Ensure the tag contains only a static template identifier + version (no customer/org/user identifiers).
  - Confirm gateway whitelist validation is strict.
- Owner / tracking:
  - Nikhil Sharma + Brian Murphy (gateway + event definitions), Monica Patel (template docs)
  - Reference: github:redwood-quickstarts/pr-115-add-template-telemetry-tags-for-adoption-metrics; slack:devex/1740741199-template-telemetry-tagging-and-privacy

P1 (must fix before launch if feasible; otherwise ship-blocking requires explicit exception)
4) Sample data realism / PII risk
- Observation:
  - Some sample docs in early drafts looked like real internal docs/customer excerpts.
  - Even if synthetic, “too realistic” increases risk that users treat it as real and re-share.
- Required action:
  - All sample documents must be:
    - Clearly synthetic and labeled as such.
    - Free of names/addresses/phone numbers/emails that could look real.
    - Small (size-limited) to reduce accidental ingestion into logging.
  - Add a prominent README note: “Do not ingest or log PII. Review your data handling before production.”
- Owner:
  - Wei Chen (content), Marcus Lin (review)

5) Secrets handling in README and code comments
- Observation:
  - Several docs mention “export REDWOOD_API_KEY=…” without reinforcing best practices.
  - Risk that users commit .env files or paste keys into shell history.
- Required action:
  - README must include:
    - Use of environment variables via .env.example only; .env should be gitignored.
    - Guidance for secret managers in production (AWS/GCP/Azure, Kubernetes secrets).
    - Explicit warning to never hardcode keys in source.
  - Ensure template repos include:
    - .gitignore entries for .env, *.pem, local-db files, otel collector local credentials.
    - Optional pre-commit secret scan note (not mandatory for users, but recommended).
- Owner:
  - Monica Patel + Wei Chen

P2 (strongly recommended; can land shortly after launch)
6) CLI “doctor” outbound request details
- Observation:
  - Doctor checks API connectivity. Error output could include URL with query params, or inadvertently show headers.
- Recommended action:
  - Standardize error output:
    - Print request_id (if available), status code, and sanitized endpoint host.
    - Do not print full response body by default.
    - Provide a “--verbose” mode that still sanitizes secrets.
- Owner:
  - Connor O’Brien + Sahana Iyer (CLI + CI), Marcus Lin (review)

7) Dependency pinning + Renovate policy alignment
- Observation:
  - Need to comply with template dependency supply chain policy: pinned deps, scanning, and controlled upgrades.
- Recommended action:
  - Ensure:
    - Python requirements are pinned (incl. transitive where feasible) or use lockfile (uv/pip-tools/poetry) with committed lock.
    - TypeScript uses lockfile checked in (package-lock/yarn.lock/pnpm-lock) and CI verifies it.
    - Renovate PRs are grouped and require passing security scan + smoke tests.

  - linear:ENG-4190-template-vulnerability-scanning-and-dependency-pinning

- Logs must be structured (JSON) with consistent field names:
  - level, timestamp, message, service, template_name, template_version, environment (dev/prod), request_id

- Required for launch:

Doc purpose
- Capture security review notes for the Quickstart Templates (RAG + Agents) and template CLI before public launch.
- Track decisions + required actions for secrets handling, sample data, logging redaction, telemetry privacy, and Secure SDLC compliance.

Context / scope
- In scope (v1 launch):
  - Python RAG+agent template
  - TypeScript RAG+agent template
  - Template CLI (init + doctor)
  - Local dev OTel collector compose + example exporters
  - README / docs guidance that ships with templates
- Out of scope (v1):
  - Multi-tenant production collector deployment guidance beyond minimal notes
  - Enterprise-specific KMS/HSM integration examples (docs can mention, but don’t implement)

Review sessions
- 2025-02-14: initial review kickoff (DevEx + Security)
  - Attendees: Marcus Lin, Monica Patel, Kaitlyn Nguyen, Hanae Suzuki
- 2025-02-24: follow-up on redaction + telemetry tagging approach
  - Attendees: Marcus Lin, Allison Grant, Monica Patel, Wei Chen
- 2025-03-07: supply chain + CI gates review
  - Attendees: Marcus Lin, Sahana Iyer, Monica Patel

High-level outcome
- Status: Conditional approval pending closure of P0/P1 items below.
- Summary: No fundamental blockers to shipping templates, but we must tighten defaults and docs to avoid: (a) accidental secret exposure via logs/traces, (b) users copying insecure patterns from sample code, and (c) dependency drift and unscanned updates.

Threat model (quick pass)
- Primary risks:
  1) Secrets exposure (API keys, tokens, cookies) via logs, tracing attributes, crash dumps, or CLI output.
  2) PII leakage into telemetry (prompt, retrieved docs, tool inputs/outputs) via logs/traces/metrics.
  3) Supply chain issues: template dependencies + transitive deps pulling in known CVEs or compromised packages.
  4) Unsafe sample data / sample documents that appear “real” and get re-shared.
  5) “Doctor” command making outbound requests and printing overly-detailed errors (headers, full URLs, tokens).
- Non-goals (for v1): preventing users from logging their own data in their app. We can provide strong defaults, warnings, and safe scaffolding.

P0 (must fix before public launch)
1) Secrets in logs (explicit + implicit)
- Observation:
  - Several error paths (HTTP client exceptions, SDK errors) can include request headers or full exception text. Some libraries include headers by default if not carefully handled.
  - In early internal beta, at least one stack trace printed config values when env var parsing failed.
- Required action:
  - Implement a redaction filter at the logger layer that:
    - Redacts values for keys matching common secret patterns: *key, *token, authorization, cookie, set-cookie, x-api-key, bearer, password, secret.
    - Redacts values that match likely API key formats (heuristic/regex) in free-form strings.
  - Ensure exception logging uses structured fields and does NOT dump entire request objects.
  - Ensure CLI never prints environment variable values; only prints presence/absence and last-4 (optional) for debugging.
- Owner / tracking:
  - Monica Patel + Dana Lo (implementation), Marcus Lin (review)
  - Reference: github:redwood-quickstarts/pr-102-add-otel-instrumentation-and-log-redaction

2) OTel tracing attributes may contain PII
- Observation:
  - Template instrumentation initially included "prompt" and "retrieved_context" in span attributes for debugging.
  - This is not acceptable as a default. Even with local dev, users will copy/paste.
- Required action:
  - Remove all raw prompt, completion, retrieved document content, and tool payloads from default traces.
  - Replace with safe metadata only:
    - model name/version, request_id, region, http status, retry_count, streaming_reconnects
    - token counts (input/output), latency breakdown
    - retrieval stats (num_chunks, top_k, similarity score summary) WITHOUT content
    - tool name, tool success/failure, tool duration; tool argument keys allowed but values redacted
  - Provide an explicit opt-in debug mode (REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_DEBUG_TELEMETRY=true) that still does NOT record raw content by default, but can add limited hashes/lengths.
- Notes:
  - If we ever add “prompt hash”, it must be salted per-process, not stable across runs, and documented clearly.
- Owner / tracking:
  - Allison Grant (standards), Monica Patel (template changes), Marcus Lin (sign-off)

3) Template telemetry tagging must be opt-in
- Observation:
  - Adoption KPI plan includes tagging template-originated traffic via headers/tags.
  - Some corporate proxies reject unknown headers; also privacy review requires explicit user consent.
- Required action:
  - Telemetry header/tagging must be:
    - Disabled by default.
    - Explicitly enabled via env var (e.g., REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_TELEMETRY_OPT_IN=true).
    - Documented in README as “optional, non-sensitive, used for product metrics”.
  - Ensure the tag contains only a static template identifier + version (no customer/org/user identifiers).
  - Confirm gateway whitelist validation is strict.
- Owner / tracking:
  - Nikhil Sharma + Brian Murphy (gateway + event definitions), Monica Patel (template docs)
  - Reference: github:redwood-quickstarts/pr-115-add-template-telemetry-tags-for-adoption-metrics; slack:devex/1740741199-template-telemetry-tagging-and-privacy

P1 (must fix before launch if feasible; otherwise ship-blocking requires explicit exception)
4) Sample data realism / PII risk
- Observation:
  - Some sample docs in early drafts looked like real internal docs/customer excerpts.
  - Even if synthetic, “too realistic” increases risk that users treat it as real and re-share.
- Required action:
  - All sample documents must be:
    - Clearly synthetic and labeled as such.
    - Free of names/addresses/phone numbers/emails that could look real.
    - Small (size-limited) to reduce accidental ingestion into logging.
  - Add a prominent README note: “Do not ingest or log PII. Review your data handling before production.”
- Owner:
  - Wei Chen (content), Marcus Lin (review)

5) Secrets handling in README and code comments
- Observation:
  - Several docs mention “export REDWOOD_API_KEY=…” without reinforcing best practices.
  - Risk that users commit .env files or paste keys into shell history.
- Required action:
  - README must include:
    - Use of environment variables via .env.example only; .env should be gitignored.
    - Guidance for secret managers in production (AWS/GCP/Azure, Kubernetes secrets).
    - Explicit warning to never hardcode keys in source.
  - Ensure template repos include:

title: Launchpad POC: Stakeholder Alignment & SLA Playbook
preview:
Purpose:
This is a lightweight, customer-facing POC launch playbook intended to: align stakeholders on business goals and success criteria; set measurable SLAs and acceptance tests; document operational constraints; and provide a clear onramp checklist for Redwood and the customer engineering teams.

POC at-a-glance:
- Customer: HelioFinance (payments / fraud detection using LLM-driven classification + enrichment)
- POC window: 2025-01-08 -> 2025-02-05 (4 weeks)
- Scope: Real-time transaction enrichment and fraud risk tagging for a slice of traffic (sandbox + shadow mode)
- Redwood deliverables: hosted inference endpoint, eval harness, telemetry dashboard, baseline rollout policy, and a freeze of model versioning during evaluation.

Business objectives (what success looks like):
1) Precision uplift: LLM-derived enrichment reduces manual review volume by >= 35% on flagged transactions (measured vs. baseline rule-only pipeline).
2) Time-to-value: Integrated pipeline in shadow mode processing sample traffic within 7 calendar days of kickoff.
3) Operational readiness: Production-worthy latency and availability profile (see technical SLAs).

Technical SLAs (targets for success / acceptance):
- Latency (end-to-end for enrichment call, 95th percentile): <= 250 ms for payloads <= 512 tokens.
- Availability: >= 99.5% over the evaluation window (excluding scheduled maintenance windows).
- Throughput: sustain 200 requests/sec for the test traffic shape; observe graceful degradation beyond.
- Cost target: average per-1k-token inference cost <= $0.60 (hosted + infra).
- Quality: model-side confidence calibration such that predicted low-confidence cases match manual-review rates within ±10% of expected.

Acceptance tests (pass/fail criteria and how we measure):
- Integration smoke: sample webhook flow end-to-end (payload -> Redwood -> enrichment -> validation) executed successfully 100 consecutive times.
- Latency test: run load test at 1x and 2x expected RPS for 30 minutes; compute p95 latency, must meet target at 1x and demonstrate graceful degradation at 2x (no hard timeouts).
- Accuracy test: apply 1k labeled transactions (holdout set). If enrichment labels match gold labels with >= 78% F1 (weighted) -> pass.
- Cost run: replay 10k requests with representative token shapes to project per-1k token cost; must be <= target.
- Security review: completion of customer-provided security checklist and signed page in the nexus security doc.

Milestones and timeline (high level):
- Day 0 (Kickoff): stakeholder align, access checklist, scope confirmation.
- Days 1-3: Redwood infra provisioning + customer API credentials + test dataset ingestion.
- Week 1: Integration and smoke tests.
- Week 2: Load/latency runs and initial quality evals.
- Week 3: Iteration on prompt templates, caching config, and quantization knobs.
- Week 4: Final evaluation, acceptance run, handoff plus recommendations for Dedicated/Private.

Roles & responsibilities (owners):
- Redwood SE lead: Marcus Lin — POC lead, runbook owner, primary customer contact.
- Redwood infra/ops: Aisha Gomez — telemetry, endpoint provisioning, SLI collection.
- Product/Model liaison: Tomoko Watanabe — prompt/version control, model selection.
- Customer eng lead: Priya Nair (customer) — dataset, API keys, stakeholder coordination.
- Customer security: Diego Rivera — security validation and audit logistics.

Workshop agenda (initial kickoff session — 60 minutes):
1) Introductions & objectives (10m)
2) Customer business flows + baseline pain points (10m)
3) POC scope, dataset, and privacy constraints (10m)
4) Acceptance criteria & SLAs walkthrough (15m)
5) Immediate access checklist + next steps (10m)
6) Q&A & parking lot items (5m)

Access & data requirements checklist (must be provided before Day 3):
- Sandbox API endpoint (read-only) for transaction samples (TLS + token).
- Representative CSV/Parquet labeled sample set (min 10k rows, column mapping).
- Example payloads for production webhook.
- Network allowlist entries for Redwood hosted IPs (if needed).
- Contact for security questionnaire and SOC2/ISO attachments.

Operational runbook excerpts (quick start):
- Deployment: use Redwood hosted endpoint id: RLW-helm-xxxx (provisioned by Aisha).
- Canary policy: 10% shadow -> 50% shadow -> 100% shadow (no writeback) during POC; writeback only after explicit acceptance.
- Caching: enable prefix cache for repeated invoice descriptions (TTL 30m).
- Retries: 2 retries with exponential backoff; fail fast if model returns empty output.

Risk register & mitigations (top 5):
1) Latency spike under stateful caching pressure — Mitigate: pre-warm KV cache and enforce token length caps.
2) Quality drift with longer retrospection windows — Mitigate: lock prompt + model version during measurement window.
3) Data privacy concerns — Mitigate: honor data retention clause; use redaction pipeline for PII.
4) Unexpected cost overrun — Mitigate: set hard quota alerting and circuit breaker on spend.
5) Production dependencies misconfigured (webhook/auth) — Mitigate: Day-1 connectivity test and fallback mock endpoint.

Evaluation & instrumentation plan (what we instrument, where we put dashboards):
- Metrics: request count, p50/p95 latency, model tokens per request, per-request cost, error rate, model confidence histogram, labeled quality metrics (precision/recall/F1).
- Dashboards: per-route latency/cost, success/failure traces, sample outputs for manual inspection (redwood console + shared Grafana link).
- Alerting: p95 latency > 300ms for 5m, error rate > 2% for 10m, cost burn > 120% of projection in 24h.

Handoff checklist & post-POC recommendation notes:
- If acceptance passes: recommended next step is a 3-month Reserved Capacity (Dedicated) pilot with a phased SLA uplift and an integration of CI evals into customer repo.
- If acceptance fails on quality but meets ops SLAs: propose a focused prompt engineering sprint + dataset augmentation attempt (2 weeks).
- If acceptance fails on ops SLAs: ops remediation plan (autoscaling, region routing) + re-run.

Immediate next actions (owners and due dates):
- Provide sandbox dataset and API keys -> Customer (Priya) by 2025-01-10.
- Provision hosted endpoint and send credentials -> Aisha by 2025-01-11.
- Schedule Day-0 kickoff (calendar invite) -> Marcus by 2025-01-08.
- Create evaluation JIRA board and link tickets -> Marcus by 2025-01-08 (JIRA-9342).

Open questions / parking lot:
- Can customer relax token caps for 5% of requests during a stress run? (Priya to confirm)
- Are there additional compliance documents required beyond SOC2? (Diego to confirm)

Notes for the workshop facilitator:
- Keep the kickoff tight — focus on measurable outcomes, not feature backlogs.
- If the customer asks about model ownership or private deployment, defer to the tailored recommendation section post acceptance.
- Capture one “customer-risk” artifact per meeting and add to the risk register.

Appendix: quick links and references:
- Confluence starter checklist: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SE/Launchpad-POC-Template
- Example prompt templates (internal): /repos/prompt-templates/helio-finance.md
- Evaluation harness PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/poc-scripts/pull/58

Document history:
- 2024-11-05 Marcus Lin created initial draft.
- 2024-11-12 Sofia added security checklist section.
- 2025-02-12 Tomoko updated SLA targets after infra sizing call.

4) Acceptance criteria & SLAs walkthrough (15m)
5) Immediate access checklist + next steps (10m)
6) Q&A & parking lot items (5m)

Access & data requirements checklist (must be provided before Day 3):
- Sandbox API endpoint (read-only) for transaction samples (TLS + token).
- Representative CSV/Parquet labeled sample set (min 10k rows, column mapping).
- Example payloads for production webhook.
- Network allowlist entries for Redwood hosted IPs (if needed).
- Contact for security questionnaire and SOC2/ISO attachments.

Operational runbook excerpts (quick start):
- Deployment: use Redwood hosted endpoint id: RLW-helm-xxxx (provisioned by Aisha).
- Canary policy: 10% shadow -> 50% shadow -> 100% shadow (no writeback) during POC; writeback only after explicit acceptance.
- Caching: enable prefix cache for repeated invoice descriptions (TTL 30m).
- Retries: 2 retries with exponential backoff; fail fast if model returns empty output.

Risk register & mitigations (top 5):
1) Latency spike under stateful caching pressure — Mitigate: pre-warm KV cache and enforce token length caps.
2) Quality drift with longer retrospection windows — Mitigate: lock prompt + model version during measurement window.
3) Data privacy concerns — Mitigate: honor data retention clause; use redaction pipeline for PII.
4) Unexpected cost overrun — Mitigate: set hard quota alerting and circuit breaker on spend.
5) Production dependencies misconfigured (webhook/auth) — Mitigate: Day-1 connectivity test and fallback mock endpoint.

Evaluation & instrumentation plan (what we instrument, where we put dashboards):
- Metrics: request count, p50/p95 latency, model tokens per request, per-request cost, error rate, model confidence histogram, labeled quality metrics (precision/recall/F1).
- Dashboards: per-route latency/cost, success/failure traces, sample outputs for manual inspection (redwood console + shared Grafana link).
- Alerting: p95 latency > 300ms for 5m, error rate > 2% for 10m, cost burn > 120% of projection in 24h.

Handoff checklist & post-POC recommendation notes:
- If acceptance passes: recommended next step is a 3-month Reserved Capacity (Dedicated) pilot with a phased SLA uplift and an integration of CI evals into customer repo.
- If acceptance fails on quality but meets ops SLAs: propose a focused prompt engineering sprint + dataset augmentation attempt (2 weeks).
- If acceptance fails on ops SLAs: ops remediation plan (autoscaling, region routing) + re-run.

Immediate next actions (owners and due dates):
- Provide sandbox dataset and API keys -> Customer (Priya) by 2025-01-10.
- Provision hosted endpoint and send credentials -> Aisha by 2025-01-11.
- Schedule Day-0 kickoff (calendar invite) -> Marcus by 2025-01-08.
- Create evaluation JIRA board and link tickets -> Marcus by 2025-01-08 (JIRA-9342).

Open questions / parking lot:
- Can customer relax token caps for 5% of requests during a stress run? (Priya to confirm)
- Are there additional compliance documents required beyond SOC2? (Diego to confirm)

Notes for the workshop facilitator:
- Keep the kickoff tight — focus on measurable outcomes, not feature backlogs.
- If the customer asks about model ownership or private deployment, defer to the tailored recommendation section post acceptance.
- Capture one “customer-risk” artifact per meeting and add to the risk register.

Appendix: quick links and references:
- Confluence starter checklist: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SE/Launchpad-POC-Template
- Example prompt templates (internal): /repos/prompt-templates/helio-finance.md
- Evaluation harness PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/poc-scripts/pull/58
